Q1: I tried to set up a consistent routine, dedicating around three to four hours most days to studying, but honestly it rarely worked out as planned. Being 28 and having other responsibilities, I found myself squeezing study time into whatever gaps I could find. I'm a visual learner, so I spent a lot of time creating diagrams and charts from the lecture content, which took longer than just reading. My routine looked organized on paper, but in practice it was more scattered than I'd like to admit.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied more than I wanted it to. I'd start each week with good intentions to study at set times, but then deadlines or work obligations would throw everything off. When exams or assignments were coming up, I'd cram more hours in, but during quieter weeks my motivation just wasn't there. The anxiety about falling behind made it hard to stick to any consistent schedule because I'd either overwork myself or avoid studying altogether.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or that gnawing feeling of anxiety that I was missing something important. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if new materials had been posted, even if I didn't plan to study right then. The fear of being caught off guard by an announcement or assignment change kept me checking in, even when I wasn't in the right headspace to actually engage with the content.
Q4: There were a few weeks early on where logging in became almost automatic, usually in the morning with my coffee. But that habit didn't stick once the workload from my eight online courses started piling up. The mindfulness exercises I started doing helped me approach the LMS with less dread, but I wouldn't say it ever became a comfortable routine. It felt more like a necessary obligation than a natural habit.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the visual content. I watched almost 200 minutes of video content because that format just clicks with my brain better than reading dense text. The discussion forums were also helpful when other students explained concepts in different ways, giving me alternative perspectives that sometimes made things clearer than the original materials.
Q6: I often skipped the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs because they felt overwhelming and text-heavy. My assignment completion was pretty low because I'd open them, feel that wave of anxiety, and then convince myself I'd come back to them later. The resources that didn't have clear visual components or seemed too abstract got pushed aside, which I know hurt my performance in the end.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to what would be on the exam or in assignments. If something had clear visual explanations or step-by-step breakdowns, I was much more likely to engage with it fully. I also valued activities where I could interact with peers because those 46 peer interactions I had actually helped reduce my anxiety about the material.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. With the anxiety I was dealing with, I had to triage everything, and optional stuff always fell to the bottom of the list. Required activities carried this weight of fear about consequences, which paradoxically sometimes made me avoid them too. The optional ones felt less threatening but also less urgent, so they rarely got done.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then work through them on my tablet while commuting or during breaks at work. I also used external YouTube videos and other educational sites to supplement the course content, especially when I needed concepts explained visually in different ways. My handwritten notes and diagrams probably represent hours of work that the system never captured.
Q10: There were many times I'd print out materials or export them to my tablet and study for hours without any LMS activity showing. I'd also discuss concepts with a study group I formed with other students, working through problems together in person. Sometimes I'd watch a video once online, then replay my downloaded copy multiple times while taking detailed visual notes. The LMS only saw that initial view, not the real depth of my engagement.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my primary study method for a lot of the course. I'd access a lecture video or PDF once, download it, and then work with it offline for days. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so having offline copies was essential. I'd annotate PDFs, create mind maps from the content, and review them repeatedly without ever logging back into the system.
Q12: Around mid-semester, my anxiety really spiked and it became incredibly hard to even open the LMS. I felt scared about how far behind I was getting, which created this vicious cycle of avoidance. The workload from managing eight online courses simultaneously became overwhelming, and I started feeling paralyzed rather than motivated. That's when I started the mindfulness intervention, which helped my mood but couldn't fully reverse the damage to my grades.
Q13: The fatigue was real, especially trying to balance everything at 28 with more responsibilities than typical students. When I was exhausted, I'd log in with good intentions but just scroll through without actually processing anything. Confusion about certain topics made me spend time on external resources instead of the LMS, and competing responsibilities meant I often chose the most urgent task rather than the most important one for my learning.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open while my mind was racing with anxious thoughts about everything I needed to do. Sometimes I'd watch videos at 2x speed just to mark them as complete, not really absorbing anything. There were sessions where I'd click through materials in a fog, physically present but mentally checked out due to stress and overwhelm.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were crucial for my grade. The fear of doing them poorly made starting feel impossible, so I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material better first. This avoidance behavior was definitely tied to my anxiety, and even though the mindfulness helped me recognize the pattern, breaking it was another matter entirely. My 14.7% assignment completion rate really reflects this struggle.
Q16: Deadlines created these intense bursts of activity followed by periods of exhaustion and avoidance. I'd cram everything into the last possible hours, which meant my LMS activity probably looked very spiky rather than consistent. The pressure of deadlines actually increased my anxiety rather than motivating me productively. I'd sometimes miss deadlines entirely because the fear of submitting imperfect work paralyzed me.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost, but critical feedback often sent me into an anxiety spiral that made me avoid that area of the course. I'd delay checking grades because I was scared of what I'd find. When I did receive constructive feedback, I genuinely wanted to improve, but the emotional weight of criticism made it hard to engage with it productively.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with the amount of content and the pace of new material being released. Having eight courses running simultaneously meant I couldn't give this one the attention it deserved. The structure didn't account for students like me who need more time to process information visually. I would have benefited from more scaffolded, smaller chunks of content rather than large modules.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it suited my visual learning style. However, the sheer volume of materials and the way assignments were weighted made engagement harder. The discussion forums were helpful when active, but sometimes they felt like another obligation rather than a support. Clearer visual roadmaps of what was expected each week would have helped me manage my anxiety better.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, honestly. You can spend hours watching videos, participating in discussions, and clicking through materials while still failing to complete the actual assignments. The anxiety of submitting work can be separate from the anxiety of engaging with content. Sometimes high activity is actually a form of productive procrastination, where you feel busy but avoid the things that really count.
Q21: I can see how that happens, though it wasn't quite my experience. Some students probably study more efficiently offline or have stronger foundational knowledge. They might download everything once and master it without needing to return to the LMS repeatedly. My issue was that even with decent engagement in some areas, my assignment completion and exam performance didn't reflect the time I actually spent studying.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when you're active and what resources you access, which gives some insight into habits. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline work students do. They can't capture whether someone is anxiously clicking through materials versus deeply learning. They also miss the emotional state behind the behavior, like whether someone is scared and avoiding or confident and efficient.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to check boxes. It's about feeling connected to the content and seeing how it matters beyond just passing the course. For me, real engagement happened in those moments when I was creating visual summaries and discussing concepts with peers, even though much of that wasn't captured by the LMS. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just time spent logged in.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much my anxiety affected every interaction I had with the course. It can't capture the hours I spent studying offline, the peer discussions, or the visual notes I created. It wouldn't show that my low activity sometimes meant I was paralyzed by fear, not disinterested. And it definitely wouldn't show that the mindfulness intervention genuinely helped my mood and approach, even if my grades didn't fully recover in time.
